title: Harnessing a 256-qubit Neutral Atom Simulator for Graph Classification
authors: Edoardo Giusto, Gabriele Iurlaro, Bartolomeo Montrucchio, Alberto Scionti, Olivier Terzo, Chiara Vercellino, Giacomo Vitali, Paolo Viviani
abstract: Neutral atom platforms are analogue quantum simulators that offer the possibility to map graphs onto a 2D qubit register using programmable Rubidium atoms arrays, whose valence electrons' energy state is used as qubits, using optical tweezers. This makes it possible to implement algorithms for solving graph combinatorial optimization and Quantum Machine Learning (QML) tasks, such as graph classification. However, the restrictions of real hardware, as well as the very low number of publicly available machines, make such implementation non-trivial. In this work, we manage to compute the Quantum Evolution Kernel (QEK) to extract the features from graphs of the PROTEINS dataset using the 256-qubits Aquila platform (available through AWS) and then we apply classical Machine Learning (ML) techniques for the final classification. The method is benchmarked against classical kernels, resulting in slightly better performance, proving the effectiveness of the method, even in the case of a noisy quantum simulator.
summary: This paper demonstrates a successful implementation of a quantum machine learning algorithm for graph classification using a 256-qubit neutral atom simulator. By optimizing the quantum pulse sequence via Bayesian optimization, the researchers achieved performance that exceeds classical benchmarks on protein datasets. The work highlights the potential of using programmable Rydberg atom arrays for real-world quantum computing tasks despite current hardware noise.
main_problem: Implementing Quantum Machine Learning (QML) for graph classification on real-world hardware, specifically overcoming the constraints of noise and limited connectivity in neutral atom simulators.
main_result: The optimized Quantum Evolution Kernel (QEK) on the 256-qubit Aquila platform achieved an F1 score of 46.3% on the PROTEINS12 dataset, outperforming both classical Shortest Path Kernels and unoptimized quantum protocols.
method: A hybrid quantum-classical pipeline using a Quantum Evolution Kernel (QEK) based on Jensen-Shannon divergence, optimized via Bayesian Optimization to find the best pulse sequence parameters.
model_or_system: A 256-qubit neutral atom simulator (QuEra Aquila) using Rubidium atoms trapped in optical tweezers, where qubits are defined by Rydberg and ground states and interactions are governed by the Rydberg blockade and an Ising Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Energy distributions derived from atom measurements and the resulting probability distributions used for kernel computation.
important_parameters: Pulse parameters (Rabi frequency, detuning, and duration), kernel scaling factor (mu), and the number of measurement shots.
main_assumptions: Graphs must be representable as Unit Disk Graphs to fit the physical connectivity of the atom array; the study assumes the effectiveness of the DEN model for graph embedding.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the layered time evolution of the Hamiltonian; Figure 2 shows dataset statistics; Figure 3 illustrates graph embedding onto the register; Figure 4 is a flowchart of the hybrid approach; Figure 5 compares energy distributions; Figure 6 shows a kernel matrix heatmap; Figure 7 compares optimized vs. non-optimized waveforms; Figure 8 compares energy distributions under different parameters.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the QML problem for graph classification, details the physical platform and hardware constraints, describes the graph embedding and QEK algorithm, presents the Bayesian optimization of waveforms, and concludes with performance benchmarking against classical methods and noise analysis.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a practical application of Rydberg-based quantum simulators for complex tasks like graph classification and shows how pulse optimization can mitigate hardware noise in many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Layered time evolution, highlighting the order of application of the Hamiltonians and the parameters Λ.
Fig. 2: Nodes and edges distribution in PROTEINS dataset.
Fig. 3: example of initial not-UD graph (top) and the asso- ciated UD embedding produced by the modified DEN model (bottom). The discrete row constraint of Aquila is visible.
Fig. 4: The full Hybrid quantum-classical emulation approach.
Fig. 5: Example of obtained energy distributions for two different graphs.
